skill.name: evaluation
skill.description: Build evaluation frameworks for agent systems
skill.summary: Build evaluation frameworks for agent systems
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 29 static findings are false positives. The skill is documentation-only with Python code examples. External commands detection was triggered by markdown code block formatting. Network detection was triggered by a source URL in YAML frontmatter. Weak cryptographic algorithm detection was triggered by the word 'token' (LLM tokens). System/network reconnaissance detection was triggered by the word 'exploration' in evaluation methodology context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Block Formatting (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner detected backticks at lines 150, 158, 164 which are Python code examples in markdown, not shell execution

dangerous_patterns.title: Source URL in YAML Frontmatter (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Line 4 contains a source URL in the skill metadata, not an active network call

dangerous_patterns.title: Token Usage in LLM Context (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple lines mention 'token' in the context of LLM token usage, not cryptographic tokens

dangerous_patterns.title: Exploration in Evaluation Context (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Lines 30, 52, 53, 211, 213 discuss agent exploration of different paths for testing, not network reconnaissance

content.value_statement: Agents require different evaluation approaches than traditional software. This skill provides frameworks to measure agent quality, track improvements, and validate context engineering choices.
actual_capabilities: Design multi-dimensional evaluation rubrics covering factual accuracy, completeness, and tool efficiency
Implement LLM-as-judge evaluation prompts for scalable assessment
Build test sets stratified by complexity from simple queries to extended interactions
Create automated evaluation pipelines that run on agent changes
Set up continuous monitoring with quality alerts for production systems
Evaluate context engineering choices by comparing different context strategies
limitations: Does not execute agents - only provides evaluation methodology and examples
Does not include pre-built test datasets - users must create their own
Does not provide runtime agent monitoring infrastructure
Cannot guarantee evaluation accuracy for highly subjective tasks

use_cases.title: Build Quality Gates for Agent Pipelines
use_cases.description: Create evaluation frameworks that run automatically before agent deployment to catch regressions and ensure minimum quality thresholds.

use_cases.title: Compare Agent Configurations
use_cases.description: Systematically compare different agent architectures, model choices, and context strategies using structured evaluation to identify optimal configurations.

use_cases.title: Validate Context Engineering Choices
use_cases.description: Test different context strategies, degradation patterns, and optimization techniques to ensure context engineering decisions improve agent quality.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Agent Response Evaluation
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick evaluation of a single agent response

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Agent Responses
Evaluate Extended Interaction
Generate Evaluation Report

output_examples.input:
Task: Answer user question about recent AI research
Agent Output: 'Recent advances include... [accurate summary of 3 papers with citations]'
Rubric: factual_accuracy=0.9, completeness=0.8, citation_accuracy=0.95
output_examples.output:
Evaluation Result:
- Factual Accuracy: 0.9 (Claims match ground truth)
- Completeness: 0.8 (Covers key aspects but missing one area)
- Citation Accuracy: 0.95 (All citations valid)
- Overall Score: 0.88
- Status: PASS (threshold: 0.7)

output_examples.input:
Test set with 4 complexity levels:
- Simple: factual lookup
- Medium: comparative analysis
- Complex: multi-step reasoning
- Very Complex: research synthesis
output_examples.output:
Complexity Stratification Results:
- Simple: 92% pass rate (23/25)
- Medium: 85% pass rate (17/20)
- Complex: 68% pass rate (17/25)
- Very Complex: 45% pass rate (9/20)
Insight: Performance degrades significantly above 'complex' level

output_examples.input:
Compare context strategies: Full context vs Summarized vs RAG
Same test set of 50 queries
output_examples.output:
Context Strategy Comparison:
| Strategy    | Avg Score | Tokens Used | Efficiency |
| Full        | 0.85      | 45,000     | 0.019      |
| Summarized  | 0.72      | 12,000     | 0.060      |
| RAG         | 0.78      | 8,000      | 0.098      |
Recommendation: RAG provides best efficiency for factual tasks

best_practices: Start with small test sets during development - early agent changes show large effects
Use multi-dimensional rubrics rather than single metrics to capture agent quality
Supplement automated LLM evaluation with human review for edge cases
Track evaluation metrics over time to identify trends and regressions
Set pass thresholds based on your specific use case requirements, not arbitrary numbers
anti_patterns: Evaluating specific execution paths instead of outcomes - agents may find valid alternative routes
Using only simple test cases - complex interactions reveal different failure modes
Ignoring token efficiency - unlimited context masks real-world performance issues
Setting thresholds too high and blocking all agent changes, or too low and releasing poor quality

faq.question: What is LLM-as-judge and how does it work?
faq.answer: LLM-as-judge uses a language model to evaluate agent outputs rather than human reviewers. You provide the task, agent output, evaluation criteria, and the judge model returns structured scores and feedback. It scales to large test sets while maintaining consistent evaluation standards.

faq.question: How do I determine appropriate pass/fail thresholds?
faq.answer: Set thresholds based on your use case requirements. For high-stakes applications, use 0.8 or higher. For exploratory or prototype agents, 0.5-0.6 may be acceptable. Start conservative and adjust based on production quality feedback.

faq.question: How many test cases do I need?
faq.answer: Start with 20-50 cases during development. Early in agent development, small samples reveal large effects. Expand to hundreds of cases before production deployment. Sample from real usage patterns and include known edge cases.

faq.question: Can this skill execute agents for evaluation?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides evaluation methodology, rubric designs, and code examples. You integrate it with your existing agent execution framework to run evaluations automatically.

faq.question: How do I handle non-deterministic agent behavior?
faq.answer: Run each test case multiple times (3-5 iterations) to capture variance. Report both average scores and standard deviation. Accept that some variation is normal for agent systems and focus on overall trends.

faq.question: What dimensions should my evaluation rubric include?
faq.answer: At minimum: factual accuracy, completeness, and tool efficiency. Add citation accuracy for research tasks, coherence for multi-turn interactions, and efficiency metrics when token usage matters. Weight dimensions based on your use case priorities.
